skill.name: pptx
skill.description: Create, edit, inspect, validate, and convert PowerPoint presentations using HTML-to-PPTX, OOXML, thumbnail, and replacement workflows.
skill.summary: Work with PowerPoint decks through guided PPTX creation, editing, validation, and analysis workflows.
skill.author: DYAI2025

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many false positives from markdown examples, JavaScript template literals, and OOXML schema terms. However, multiple scripts extract user-supplied Office archives with extractall(), which creates a credible Zip Slip filesystem write risk. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent was found, so this should be remediated before publication rather than blocked as malware.

high_findings.title: Unsafe Archive Extraction of User-Supplied Office Files
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The unpacking and validation helpers call zipfile.extractall() on Office documents without visible member path validation. A crafted PPTX, DOCX, or XLSX archive could write files outside the intended output or temporary directory.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct extractall() calls are present on user-controlled Office archives. No path normalization or destination containment check is visible at these extraction sites.

medium_findings.title: External Document Conversion Commands
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill runs external programs such as soffice, pdftoppm, and git. Arguments are passed as lists without shell=True, which reduces injection risk, but user documents are still processed by complex external binaries.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are hardcoded and do not use a shell, so command injection is unlikely. The risk remains because untrusted documents flow into external converters and diff tools.

medium_findings.title: Privilege-Elevated Dependency Installation Guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill documentation recommends sudo apt-get install commands for LibreOffice and Poppler. This is not executed automatically, but marketplace users may follow privileged installation steps from community content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sudo commands are explicit in dependency instructions. They are documentation rather than hidden execution, so the risk depends on user action.

medium_findings.title: Environment-Controlled Temporary Directory
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The HTML conversion script reads process.env.TMPDIR to choose a temporary directory. This is not credential access, but an attacker-controlled environment could influence where browser rendering writes temporary files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable is TMPDIR, not a secret. The concern is path influence in a rendering workflow rather than exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Template Literal Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Many backtick findings are JavaScript template literals or markdown command examples. The cited examples build validation messages or document expected commands, not shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected backticks are inside JavaScript strings or markdown fences. They do not invoke a shell.

low_findings.title: Schema Keyword Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Weak-crypto, C2, and network-scanner alerts match normal OOXML schema words such as seq, arabic2Minus, and maps. No evidence found that these schema entries perform crypto, command-and-control, or scanning behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected lines are XML schema declarations and enumeration values. They are data definitions, not executable code paths.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are Mostly Documentation or Namespaces
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reviewed hardcoded URLs are license links or OOXML relationship namespace values. They do not show outbound runtime network requests or credential exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected URL locations are static references used for licensing or OOXML relationship metadata. No network API call is present in those locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: zipfile.extractall Without Zip Slip Guard
dangerous_patterns.description: The code extracts archive members directly into output or temporary directories. Replace extractall() with safe extraction that rejects absolute paths and parent traversal.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The same unsafe extraction pattern appears in multiple document-processing paths. The inputs are Office files, which are ZIP archives supplied by users.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Binary Processing of Untrusted Documents
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts pass user-selected documents to LibreOffice and Poppler utilities. This is a legitimate workflow, but it should run with sandboxing and patched dependencies.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command invocations are direct and hardcoded. The main uncertainty is exploitability, which depends on the runtime sandbox and converter versions.

content.value_statement: PowerPoint work often requires XML edits, visual checks, and reliable conversion steps. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through PPTX creation, editing, analysis, and validation.
actual_capabilities: Creates new PowerPoint decks from HTML slide layouts.
Extracts and inspects PPTX text, comments, speaker notes, themes, and slide XML.
Edits existing presentations through OOXML replacement and packaging workflows.
Generates thumbnail grids for visual review of slide decks.
Validates presentation structure and relationships using included OOXML helpers.
Documents dependencies for conversion, rendering, image processing, and secure XML parsing.
limitations: Requires local dependencies such as LibreOffice, Poppler, Playwright, and pptxgenjs for full workflows.
Complex slide animations and proprietary PowerPoint behaviors may need manual verification.
Unsafe archive extraction must be fixed before marketplace publication.
Generated decks still need visual review in PowerPoint or a compatible viewer.

use_cases.title: Build a Client Presentation
use_cases.description: Create a polished deck from a brief, then export thumbnails for quick stakeholder review.

use_cases.title: Update Existing Slides
use_cases.description: Inspect slide XML, replace text or assets, and repack the presentation with validation checks.

use_cases.title: Audit Presentation Content
use_cases.description: Extract text, comments, speaker notes, theme colors, and layout details from a PPTX file.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple Deck
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 6-slide PowerPoint deck about [topic]. Use a clear structure, readable typography, and a consistent color palette.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze a Deck
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze [presentation.pptx]. Summarize the slide titles, key messages, speaker notes, comments, and visual style.

prompt_templates.title: Edit an Existing Presentation
prompt_templates.prompt: Update [presentation.pptx] using these changes: [changes]. Preserve the existing theme, layouts, and slide order.

prompt_templates.title: Validate and Repair OOXML
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect the raw OOXML in [presentation.pptx], identify relationship or schema problems, repair them, and explain each change.

output_examples.input: Create a board update deck from quarterly metrics and product milestones.
output_examples.output: A PowerPoint deck with an executive summary, KPI slides, roadmap timeline, risk slide, and visual review thumbnails.

output_examples.input: Review a sales deck and list content gaps before tomorrow meeting.
output: Missing proof points and unclear claims
Suggested edits for speaker notes and visuals

output_examples.input: Replace all placeholder customer names and validate the final PPTX.
output_examples.output: An updated presentation package with replacements applied, relationship checks completed, and remaining manual review notes.

best_practices: Run PPTX processing in a sandbox when files come from untrusted sources.
Review generated thumbnails before sending any deck to stakeholders.
Keep source assets, edited XML, and final PPTX files in separate folders.
anti_patterns: Do not process unknown PPTX files on a sensitive workstation without sandboxing.
Do not skip visual review after automated slide generation or XML edits.
Do not install privileged dependencies from community instructions without approval.

faq.question: Can this skill create new PowerPoint files?
faq.answer: Yes. It guides HTML-to-PPTX creation with layout, typography, color, and validation steps.

faq.question: Can it edit existing decks?
faq.answer: Yes. It can inspect OOXML, replace content, update assets, and repack presentations.

faq.question: Does it support speaker notes and comments?
faq.answer: Yes. The instructions cover raw XML access for notes slides and comment files.

faq.question: Does it require extra software?
faq.answer: Some workflows require local tools such as LibreOffice, Poppler, Playwright, pptxgenjs, and Python packages.

faq.question: Is this safe for untrusted PPTX files?
faq.answer: Not without remediation and sandboxing. The audit found unsafe ZIP extraction in several scripts.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
